fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that actor concurrency library we've been talking about. The core idea is that developers should be able to spin up isolated 'systems', put named workers inside them, and have those workers talk to each other via messages without worrying about the plumbing underneath. Think of it like the pattern we used in the notification service refactor last quarter — same isolation idea, but generalized.

A few things I know are important from the stakeholder side: naming has to be strict (we had bugs before from weird characters sneaking into identifiers), parents should know about their kids but not their grandkids, and if something crashes it shouldn't take down the whole world — there should be ways to configure recovery. Also someone mentioned we need a way to 'ask' an actor something and wait for an answer, but it can't wait forever, and the error messages that come back need to be clean, nothing internal leaking out.

We also need lifecycle stuff — actors should be stoppable, stopping a parent should clean up everything below it, and there's some ask about actors that shut themselves down if they've been sitting idle too long. The after-stop situation also needs a callback of some kind so you can inspect what was still pending.

Please make sure the code isn't one giant file — it got messy last time. Reference the structure notes in start.md for how to split things up.

trigger_keywords: which characters are allowed in actor names
what makes a segment name valid
what characters can I use in an address segment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A valid actor address segment must be a non-empty string composed solely of letters, digits, and the following URI-safe extra characters: hyphen (-), underscore (_), period (.), dollar sign ($), plus (+), exclamation mark (!), asterisk (*), single quote ('), open parenthesis ((), close parenthesis ()), and comma (,). Any whitespace (leading, trailing, or interior), path separators, reserved query/fragment characters (e.g. ?, #, &, =), and escape characters (e.g. %) make a name invalid. Non-text values and empty strings are also rejected.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a query has no timeout
missing timeout error format
what error is returned if timeout is not provided
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a query is issued without supplying a timeout value, the system must output the normalized error category 'error=missing_timeout' on its own line. No runtime exception class name, stack trace, or internal message suffix may appear in the output.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a query times out
timeout error output format
how is a query timeout reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a query times out (no response arrives before the deadline), the output must be two lines: 'error=query_timeout' followed by 'timeout_ms=<value>' where <value> is the timeout that was supplied. No exception class names or stack traces may appear.

trigger_keywords: what error if I query a stopped actor
querying a non-existent actor
stopped reference query failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Querying a stopped or never-existing actor reference must produce the normalized output 'error=stopped_reference'. The output must never contain runtime exception types, internal message text, or stack traces.

trigger_keywords: does stopping a parent also stop children
stop cascade behavior
what happens to grandchildren when a child is stopped
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Stopping an actor cascades the stop to every descendant of that actor (children, grandchildren, etc.), while sibling actors remain alive. Stopping the root system stops every remaining actor in the entire tree. After a reference is stopped, any attempt to spawn a child below it must be rejected with 'error=stopped_reference', and any message dispatched to a stopped actor is silently ignored.

trigger_keywords: what children does a parent see
does a parent see grandchildren
child visibility in parent context
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A parent actor's context exposes only its direct children (depth 1), not deeper descendants. Child names are reported in sorted order. The system registry also exposes direct children of the system root in sorted order, without surfacing deeper levels of the hierarchy.

trigger_keywords: duplicate child name error
what happens if I spawn two children with the same name
sibling name collision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Spawning a child with a name already used by a sibling under the same parent must be rejected with a two-line output: 'error=duplicate_child_name' followed by 'child_name=<name>'. The error must not expose runtime internals.

trigger_keywords: idle shutdown timer
actor auto-stop on inactivity
how does the idle timeout work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An actor configured with an idle shutdown period stops automatically once that period elapses with no message activity. Receiving any message before the deadline renews (resets) the idle timer, keeping the actor alive. After the idle period expires the actor is marked stopped and follows the same stopped-reference rules as an explicitly stopped actor.

trigger_keywords: after-stop callback
what context is available when an actor stops
queued messages after stop
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an actor stops, an after-stop callback is invoked with access to: the count of messages still queued behind the in-flight message, the content of the first such queued message, and the parent reference (from which the owning system identity can be read). The parent system name is reported as 'parent_system=<name>'.

trigger_keywords: supervision policy options
what recovery actions are available for a faulted actor
self supervision vs parent supervision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Self (actor-level) supervision policies include: resume (keep current state, continue processing), reset (reinitialize state from scratch), stop (mark actor stopped), and escalate (delegate fault decision to parent). Parent-level supervision policies triggered by escalation include: stop_all_children (stop every child under the parent), reset_all_children (reset every child's state), and reset_faulted_child (reset only the child that faulted while leaving peers untouched). The parent itself must remain alive under all parent-level policies.

trigger_keywords: default fault behavior
what happens when a stateful actor crashes by default
stateless actor crash default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default fault behavior differs by actor kind: a stateful actor stops automatically after a handler failure, while a stateless actor resumes (continues processing) after a handler failure. Initialization failures can be observed via a crash handler that stops the actor. These defaults apply when no explicit supervision policy is configured.

trigger_keywords: mailbox message ordering guarantee
does async processing preserve order
can a slow message be overtaken
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Mailbox ordering is strictly preserved: one message is fully processed (including any asynchronous work within its handler) before the next message begins processing. A message delayed by async work inside its handler cannot be overtaken by a later message. The listener's final recorded sequence must always equal the original send order.

trigger_keywords: initial state from actor name
context-based initialization
placeholder in initial state template
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An actor's initial state can be computed from a template string containing the placeholder '{name}'. At startup, before any message is processed, the runtime replaces '{name}' with the actor's configured name to produce the seed state. Subsequent messages extend from that computed seed.

trigger_keywords: system isolation
can two systems share actor names
namespace collision between systems
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each actor system owns its own isolated namespace. Two systems may each contain an actor with the same local name without any collision. A request-response interaction addressed within one system resolves only against that system's registry and never crosses into another system's namespace.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be organized
single file or multiple files
module structure for the project
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Because this domain involves multiple distinct responsibilities (address validation, mailbox scheduling, lifecycle management, supervision, and I/O adaptation), a single-file solution is explicitly prohibited. The codebase must use a multi-file directory structure (e.g. src/, tests/) that separates addressing/validation, mailbox scheduling, lifecycle, supervision, and the JSON I/O adapter into distinct logical modules. The core business logic must be fully decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'stop-cascade', 'default-fault-behavior', 'supervision-self', 'supervision-parent-policy', 'supervision-stop-all', 'after-stop', 'validate-name-list', 'create-child-path', 'start-systems', 'stateful-append', 'initial-state-from-name', 'promise-order', 'query', 'missing-query-timeout', 'child-registration'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the pattern we used in the notification service refactor last quarter — same isolation idea
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 (Actor System Identity and Isolation) in start.md: each ActorSystem owns its own namespace and actors with identical local names in different systems must not collide; see feature3_system_identity_and_isolation.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1 segment validation rules in start.md: the exact allowed character set (letters, digits, and URI-safe extras: - _ . $ + ! * ' ( ) ,) that must be enforced consistently across address building (Feature 2) and all spawn/routing operations; see feature1_path_name_validation.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the structure notes in start.md for how to split things up
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architecture & Engineering Constraints section of start.md, specifically constraint #1 (Scale-Driven Code Organization) and constraint #2 (Strict Separation of Concerns): the adapter layer must be separate from core domain logic, and responsibilities must be split into distinct modules

context_pointers.vague_reference: there's some ask about actors that shut themselves down if they've been sitting idle too long
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 10 (Stop and Idle Lifecycle) idle shutdown behavior in start.md and feature10_stop_and_idle_lifecycle.json: actors configured with an idle_timeout_ms stop automatically on inactivity, but receiving a message renews the timer

context_pointers.vague_reference: the after-stop situation also needs a callback of some kind so you can inspect what was still pending
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 15 (After-Stop Context) in start.md and feature15_after_stop_context.json: the after-stop callback receives queued_messages count, first_queued message content, and parent reference exposing parent_system name

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a bit outside what I can clarify from the business side — you'd want to dig into the start.md PRD and the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise contract. Is there a specific behavior or error scenario I can help translate into business terms?

feature10_stop_and_idle_lifecycle.json.description: Stop actors and systems safely, cascade stops to descendants, reject spawning below stopped references, ignore later dispatches, and stop idle actors after a configured inactivity period while allowing message activity to renew the timer.

feature11_default_fault_handling.json.description: Apply the default fault behavior: stateful actors stop after handler failure, stateless actors resume after handler failure, and initialization failures can be observed by a crash handler that stops the actor.

feature12_self_supervision_actions.json.description: Let an actor-specific crash policy resume, reset, stop, or escalate a faulted actor and expose the resulting actor state and liveness.

feature13_parent_supervision_actions.jso.description: Let a parent crash policy respond to an escalated child failure by stopping all children, resetting all children, or resetting only the faulted child while leaving unaffected actors according to the selected action.

feature14_peer_and_descendant_supervisio.description: Apply a self crash policy that stops the faulted actor, its peers, and descendants while keeping the parent alive.

feature15_after_stop_context.json.description: Run an after-stop callback with access to the parent reference and any messages still queued behind an in-flight message.

feature1_path_name_validation.json.description: Validate actor address segment names against the accepted character set and reject empty, non-text, whitespace, reserved, escape, and fragment/query characters.

cases.expected_output:
name="frog"
valid=yes
name="123-abc"
valid=yes
name="frog.path"
valid=yes
name="frog(path)"
valid=yes

feature2_child_path_creation.json.description: Build a child address path by appending valid child segments to a root system path, and report invalid child segments as normalized contract errors.

feature3_system_identity_and_isolation.j.description: Start named actor systems and keep actors with identical local names isolated by their owning system.

feature4_stateful_message_processing.jso.description: Process append messages through a stateful actor and return the accumulated state, including when the actor starts from an explicit initial state.

cases.expected_output: state=Hello World. The time has come!!

feature5_context_based_initial_state.jso.description: Create the initial state from the actor context so that the actor name can be reflected in subsequent stateful message handling.

input.template: Hello {name}! Is today not a joyous occasion?

cases.expected_output: state=Hello NamedActor! Is today not a joyous occasion? It is indeed

feature6_async_sequential_processing.jso.description: Preserve mailbox order for asynchronous stateful message handlers even when an earlier message takes longer to complete.

feature7_query_request_response.json.description: Send a request that includes a temporary reply address and resolve with the response when the actor replies before the timeout.

feature8_query_error_contracts.json.description: Normalize request-response failures when no timeout is supplied, the target is stopped, or no response arrives before the timeout window.

feature9_child_registration_and_duplicat.description: Register children under their parent, expose child names through the parent context, allow children to be created from within message handling, and reject duplicate sibling names.
